answer:
For Charles Dunoyer, the *classe industrieuse* (industrious class) comprises **all the useful (*utiles*) and productive (*productives*) classes** in society—that is, everyone engaged in labor that creates utility (*utilité*) or exchangeable value, whether by acting on material things (e.g., agriculture, manufacturing, commerce) or directly on human beings (e.g., medicine, education, arts, moral instruction, or even properly functioning government services).[^1][^2] This encompasses the entirety of society in its most advanced, "purely industrious" (*purement industrieux*) state, where subsistence derives solely from peaceful, non-coercive production and voluntary exchange, rather than spoliation, violence, privilege, or parasitism.[^3]

### Core Meaning
Dunoyer redefines "industry" (*industrie*) etymologically and economically as intelligent action to **construct utility within things or persons** (*intus-struo*), excluding fraud, conquest, or exploitation.[^4] Thus:
- Productive labor (*travail productif*) accumulates wealth as **durably realized utility** (e.g., health restored by doctors, knowledge imparted by teachers, justice enforced by magistrates, or moral habits fostered by good governance), not merely transient services.[^5]
- All honest professions qualify as "industrious" (*industrieuse*), from plowmen to statesmen, provided they enhance societal forces without harming others.[^6]
- Industry is the highest stage of civilization, where liberty flourishes because individuals develop their faculties maximally, use them morally (temperance, economy), and coexist peacefully via competition and association that *expands* rather than restricts others' activity.[^7]

### Boundaries
The *classe industrieuse* is sharply delimited against non-productive or destructive elements:
- **Excludes parasitic/dominating classes**: Masters, slaves, nobles, clergy (if privileged), monopolists, office-seekers (*place-seekers*), or governments living off tribute/taxes without equivalent utility. These "consume society's substance" via violence or iniquity, not production.[^8]
- **Excludes criminals/brigandage**: Fraud, war-for-profit, or unjust privileges (e.g., protective tariffs creating artificial rivalries). Even "industrial" actors become "knights of industry" (*chevaliers d'industrie*) if aiding tyranny or spoliation.[^9]
- **Not limited to manual/mechanical labor**: Unlike narrow views (e.g., Adam Smith), it includes "immaterial" producers (teachers, judges, artists) whose results *persist in persons* (e.g., virtues, skills).[^10]
- **Ideal vs. actual society**: Real societies (like 1820s France) mix industry with remnants of privilege; the pure *classe industrieuse* emerges only when all pretensions to unearned gain are abolished, leaving "nothing but labor and exchange."[^11]

Dunoyer's vision is utopian yet empirical: industry dissolves hierarchies, fosters peace (as in America), and maximizes liberty, but obstacles like overpopulation or vice persist even here.[^12]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, ch.13, [¶ ae8a20](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-ae8a20).

[^2]: *Ibid.*, ch.13, [full chapter](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/).

[^3]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, ch.0 (Introduction), [¶ 405ef1](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-405ef1) ("common to all classes of industrious people"); ch.11, [full chapter](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/).

[^4]: *Ibid.*, ch.11, [¶ f1f859–cd2259](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-f1f859).

[^5]: *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, ch.13, [¶ 62915e–36b13b](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-62915e).

[^6]: *Ibid.*, ch.13, [¶ a1cb96–53d1f5](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-a1cb96).

[^7]: *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, ch.11, [¶ 872da7–b115c1](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-872da7).

[^8]: *Ibid.*, ch.11, [¶ 12ca4c–9f5144](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-12ca4c); ch.10, [full chapter](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/10-liberte-compatible-avec-la-vie-des-peuples-chez-qu/translation/).

[^9]: *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, ch.13, [¶ fb8d6b–ac3ab6](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-fb8d6b).

[^10]: *Ibid.*, ch.13, [¶ 1708f9–76ee09](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-1708f9).

[^11]: *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, ch.0, [¶ ecca74](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-ecca74); ch.11, [¶ 2f49f9](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-2f49f9).

[^12]: *Ibid.*, ch.12, [full chapter](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/12-ii-obstacles-qui-sopposent-encore-a-la-liberte-dan/translation/).
